message:
connectors: add GitHub OAuth begin/complete helpers and harden rate-limit arithmetic

GitHub connector now exposes two new operations that wrap the shared
fcp-oauth state-machine helpers:

  - begin_oauth: builds an OAuth2Client for GitHub with the project's
    default scopes (repo, read:user), calls authorization_url(), and
    returns the generated authorization URL, CSRF state value, and the
    allowlisted redirect_uri back to the caller.
  - complete_oauth: parses the provider callback URL, validates the
    state parameter against the caller-supplied expected_state, and
    returns the extracted authorization_code plus the matched
    redirect_uri.

Both operations enforce a caller-provided `allowed_redirect_uris`
allowlist in every request. The redirect_uri on begin_oauth and the
redirect_uri inferred from the callback on complete_oauth are checked
against that list before any OAuth state is produced or any code is
returned. This keeps the connector from being used as a confused
deputy for arbitrary third-party redirect endpoints even if the
caller's request payload is partially attacker-controlled.

Supporting changes:

  - connectors/github/Cargo.toml: add fcp-oauth path dependency.
  - Cargo.lock: regenerate (picks up fcp-oauth for the github crate and
    absorbs fcp-policy lockfile drift from the earlier
    fcp-mesh: add fcp-policy path dependency commit).

Rate-limit retry-after arithmetic on secondary (403) and primary (429)
paths in the GitHub client previously did `retry_after_secs * 1000` on
raw u64 values parsed from the `Retry-After` header. A malicious or
misconfigured upstream returning a near-u64::MAX value would overflow
and wrap into a tiny retry window, defeating the rate-limit back-off.
The same bug existed in connectors/slack/src/error.rs (SlackError
conversion) and connectors/stripe/src/client.rs (after reading
retry-after from the response headers). Replace every site with
saturating_mul(1000) so the worst case is u64::MAX milliseconds — a
large but non-wrapping upper bound that preserves the intended back-off.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
